title: Replication Factor Compliance
dataSources: HCI replication status, data protection metrics
spl:
index=hci sourcetype="hci:replication"
| stats latest(configured_rf) as target_rf, latest(actual_rf) as current_rf, latest(rebuild_pct) as rebuild_progress by cluster_name, container
| eval compliant=if(current_rf>=target_rf, "Yes", "No")
| eval risk=case(current_rf<target_rf-1, "Data Loss Risk", current_rf<target_rf, "Reduced Resilience", 1==1, "Protected")
| table cluster_name, container, target_rf, current_rf, compliant, risk, rebuild_progress
| sort risk
description: HCI data resilience depends on maintaining the configured replication factor (RF2/RF3). Non-compliant replication means data loss risk if additional failures occur. Monitoring RF compliance is essential for data protection assurance.
value: HCI data resilience depends on maintaining the configured replication factor (RF2/RF3). Non-compliant replication means data loss risk if additional failures occur. Monitoring RF compliance is essential for data protection assurance.
implementation: Monitor replication factor status continuously. Alert immediately when actual RF drops below configured RF. Track rebuild progress to estimate time to full compliance. Monitor cluster capacity to ensure sufficient space for re-replication. Alert critically if RF drops to 1 (single copy—data loss imminent on next failure).
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: `TA-nutanix` or vendor-specific TA.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: HCI replication status, data protection metrics.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Monitor replication factor status continuously. Alert immediately when actual RF drops below configured RF. Track rebuild progress to estimate time to full compliance. Monitor cluster capacity to ensure sufficient space for re-replication. Alert critically if RF drops to 1 (single copy—data loss imminent on next failure).

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=hci sourcetype="hci:replication"
| stats latest(configured_rf) as target_rf, latest(actual_rf) as current_rf, latest(rebuild_pct) as rebuild_progress by cluster_name, container
| eval compliant=if(current_rf>=target_rf, "Yes", "No")
| eval risk=case(current_rf<target_rf-1, "Data Loss Risk", current_rf<target_rf, "Reduced Resilience", 1==1, "Protected")
| table cluster_name, container, target_rf, current_rf, compliant, risk, rebuild_progress
| sort risk
```

Understanding this SPL

**Replication Factor Compliance** — HCI data resilience depends on maintaining the configured replication factor (RF2/RF3). Non-compliant replication means data loss risk if additional failures occur. Monitoring RF compliance is essential for data protection assurance.

Documented **Data sources**: HCI replication status, data protection metrics. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `TA-nutanix` or vendor-specific TA. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: hci; **sourcetype**: hci:replication. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=hci, sourcetype="hci:replication". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by cluster_name, container** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• `eval` defines or adjusts **compliant** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **risk** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Pipeline stage (see **Replication Factor Compliance**): table cluster_name, container, target_rf, current_rf, compliant, risk, rebuild_progress
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Single value (RF compliance status), Gauge (rebuild progress), Table (non-compliant containers), Status grid (cluster RF map).
visualization: Single value (RF compliance status), Gauge (rebuild progress), Table (non-compliant containers), Status grid (cluster RF map).
